coredata.dc:title: Tetracycline and its quantum dots for recognition of Al3+ and application in milk developing cells bio-imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Granados, Jesús Alfredo Ortega
Thangarasu, Pandiyan
Singh, Narinder
Vázquez-Ramos, Jorge Manuel
coredata.dc:description: We developed a technique that detects Al3+ in milk/bio-samples, and reversibly applied to recognize tetracycline (TC) in milk, enhancing the fluorescence intensity without interference from other cations (Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Sm3+, Ag+, Na+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Zn2+ and Mn2+); the limit of detection (LOD) is found to be 0.00022 mM with r2 = 0.9439. The detection of Al3+ is tested in milk as well as in living cells (Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Debaryomyces spp.) by TC or by its quantum dots. This is consistent with the molecular orbital, revealing that the lowering of the energy of HOMO (Highly Occupied Molecular Orbital) discourages the electron transfer from HOMO of fluorophore to HOMO of excited states of Al-complex that increases the fluorescent intensity. Interestingly, carbon dots (CDs) generated from TC also recognize Al3+ as its LOD is as low as to 0.00050 mM with r2 of 0.9404.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracycline
Milk
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Confocal microscopy
Detection of Al3+
Bacterial samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recognition analysis of tetracycline with different cations
Titration of TC vs. Al3+ studies
Carbon dots derived from tetracycline as sensor for Al3+

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Fluorescence metal binding test for TC (100 μM) with different metal ions (100.0 mM) in solvent mixture H2O/CH3OH (80:20, %v/v); b) interference study for the mixture of TC [(2.0 mL, 0.1 mM) with Al3+ (100 μL, 100 mM)] by adding different cations (100 μL, 100 mM); c) Comparative intensity for the mixture of TC [(2.0 mL, 0.1 mM) + Al3+ (100 μL, 100 mM)] for the addition of all cations (100 μL, 100 mM). d) Fluorescence spectral titration of TC with Al3+: TC (2.0 mL, 0.1 mM) Vs Al3+ (10 μL, 0.0–0.08 mM): e) Job́s plot for TC (100 μM) with addition of Al3+ ion (100.0 mM) in solvent mixture (H2O/CH3OH (80:20, %v/v).

(i) Synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from tetracycline and ethylenediamine; (ii) XRD of carbon dots: a) carbon dots derived from citric acid; b) carbon dot derived from tetracycline; c) carbon dots from both from citric acid and tetracycline; (iii) HR-TEM of carbon dots (a, c) obtained with ethylenediamine and (b and d) carbon dots from tetracycline; (iv) a) Carbon dots (CDsTC) (10 μg/mL) derived from tetracycline employed as metal binding test with different metal ions (100.0 mM); b) Interference binding test for CDsTC (10 μg/mL) with different metal ions (100 μL, 100.0 mM); c) Titration studies of CDsTC (10 μg/mL) with Al3+ (10–10 μL, 0.5 mM) having Ag+ (100 μL/100 mM). All experiments were studied in solvent mixture H2O/CH3OH (80:20, %v/v).
a) Detection of Al3+ by using TC in milk sample; b) titration of TC with Al3+ in milk, c) different concentration of TC vs fluorescence intensity in milk.

Confocal microscopy analysis of Saccharomyces cerevisiae spp. cells in milk sample: Confocal images upon excitation: 405 nm; emission:450–550 nm (a) control (cells); (b) Al3+ ion; (c) TC; (d) TC + Al3+ ion; Optical microscopy under visible light (e) control (cells); (f) Al3+ ion; (g) TC; (h) TC + Al3+ ion.
Optimized geometrical structure of tetracycline, and its aluminium complex at B3LYP/DGDZVP in gaseous state.
Detection of tetracycline in milk samples employing different methods.

de Faria, Rosa, Silveira, and Figueiredo (2017)

Detection of Al3+ employing tetracycline as chemosensor

head.ce:title: Tetracycline and its quantum dots for recognition of Al3+ and application in milk developing cells bio-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Facultad de Química, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Ciudad Universitaria, Coyoacán 04510, México D. F., Mexico

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (IIT), Rupnagar, Punjab 140001, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We developed a technique that detects Al3+ in milk/bio-samples, and reversibly applied to recognize tetracycline (TC) in milk, enhancing the fluorescence intensity without interference from other cations (Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Sm3+, Ag+, Na+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Zn2+ and Mn2+); the limit of detection (LOD) is found to be 0.00022 mM with r2 = 0.9439. The detection of Al3+ is tested in milk as well as in living cells (Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Debaryomyces spp.) by TC or by its quantum dots. This is consistent with the molecular orbital, revealing that the lowering of the energy of HOMO (Highly Occupied Molecular Orbital) discourages the electron transfer from HOMO of fluorophore to HOMO of excited states of Al-complex that increases the fluorescent intensity. Interestingly, carbon dots (CDs) generated from TC also recognize Al3+ as its LOD is as low as to 0.00050 mM with r2 of 0.9404.

ce:section.ce:para: The contamination of milk products, especially the accumulation of antibiotics like tetracycline (TC) can cause allergic reactions, generating health issues like bacterial resistance to antibiotics, and it affects the performance of liver and kidney (Nudda et al., 2015). The determination of antibiotic residues in water or food becomes an imperative. Moreover, the existence of TC or OTC (oxytetracycline) in water/wastewater generated also causes significant environmental problems with increasing the risk of microbial death, and it leads an ecological imbalance in soils and water systems (Shappell et al., 2017). Therefore, the accurate measuring of TC or OTC in drinking water/wastewater, food products or biological samples would help to avoid severe environmental and clinical implications. Since antibiotics are being used to treat bacterial infections for both animals and humans, the excessive use provokes pathogenic resistance, as a result, it has instigated serious environmental issues, definitely, for the food products (Patel, Bier, Goldring, Hillen, & Costello, 2006). Thus, the usage of antibiotics needs to be limited to as low as to μg.kg−1 to avoid major adverse impacts to the biological systems. It is known that TC, which is one of the most widely used antibiotics to treat bacterial infections, has created bacterial resistance in aquaculture, animal husbandry, milk safety (Correddu, Nudda, Manca, Pulina, & Dalsgaard, 2015), and human (gastrointestinal and respiratory). The analysis of antibiotics and of trace metal ions in milk, Pizano and coworkers have contributed significantly by using immunoassay technology (Pizzano, Nicolai, Manzo, & Addeo, 2011) and antipeptide antibodies as analytical tools (Pizzano, Nicolai, & Addeo, 1998). Alternatively, the reuse of TC or OTC as chemo-sensor for the recognition of metal ions in water as well as in milk is interesting in the food safety point of view. Thus, the selective detection of various metal ions, contaminants or anions through luminescence turns to be an essential tool as it can be applied in biological and environmental samples.

Currently, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis (CE), electrochemical techniques (Ashley, Feng, & Sun, 2018), immunization (Chafer-Pericas et al., 2010), and microbiology (Kurittu, Lonnberg, Virta, & Karp, 2000) are being employed for the detection of organic contaminants. However, for the detection and quantification of antibiotics, particularly for TC or OTC in milk, it requires a pretreatment since it contains components like proteins and lipids that complicate TC determination; the pretreatment methods are: liquid-liquid extraction (SSL) (Ding, Liu, Peng, Liu, & Tang, 2018), solid-phase extraction (SPE) (Zhao, Wang, Wang, & Wang, 2018), or supercritical-fluid extraction (SFE) (Paul, Jayakumar, & Mishra, 2016) etc. However these methods consume lots of solvents, they are poorly selective besides requiring a complicated instrumentation process in addition to the generation of secondary pollutants. Presently, spectrofluorometry becomes a popular since it detects contaminants selectively with high sensitivity; besides, its operational procedure is very easy and quick. For instance, the binding of a receptor with a guest molecule produces a host-guest complex that behaves spectrally different from the individuals (receptor or guest) (Baudet, Guerra, & Piguet, 2017), so this can be used to detect cations or anions, or neutral organic molecules in environmental/food/biological samples by using fluorescence spectral techniques.

It is known that the presence of toxic aluminium ion in water can generate Alzheimer and Parkinson diseases and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis in humans (House et al., 2004), and the detection of Al3+ becomes cumbersome because of its poor coordination, strong hydration ability, and lack of spectroscopic characteristics (Abebe, Sutton, Perkins, & Shaw, 2018). Some chemo-sensors efficiently detect Al3+ ion, but the interference from other species is unavoidable during its application in real samples like living cells or in milk products. Searching of a suitable organic chemo-sensor that detects Al3+ exclusively is a tricky process. In the present study, tetracycline (TC) or its quantum dots are being employed as a chemo-sensor for the detection of Al3+ in real samples and it is interesting since most researchers have focused on antibiotics as contaminants. Moreover, the recognition of metal ions by TC can partially resolve analytical related environmental issues. So, with the fluorescence technique, TC quantum dots has been used to recognize Al3+ ion, and in the same way but reversely, the concentration of TC can be determined by adding Al3+ ion. Therefore, the dual use of TC/Al3+ in drinking water/wastewater or milk or other biological samples is significantly important from the application point of view. Furthermore, carbon dots (CDs TC) were prepared from tetracycline and used as carbon source and employed successfully to detect Al3+ ion but in the presence of Ag2+.

In the study, all chemical reagents (Sigma Aldrich) purchased were used without further purification. The bacterial strains were obtained from the Strain Bank (WFCC/WDCM-100), Faculty of Chemistry, UNAM for cell culture, and maintained in Cysteine Tryptic Agar (CTA). Cysteine Tryptic Agar medium (CTA, BIOXON) was employed for maintaining cell cultures, and Mueller Hinton (DIBICO) for inoculum dilution mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition analysis of tetracycline with different cations
ce:section.ce:para: TC has been explored as chemo-sensor for the recognition of cations. First, metal ion binding tests were employed, followed by analysis of interference from other cations in competitive medium (competitive binding test) as well as Guest–Host titration. In the metal binding study, briefly, a set of different cations (Cd2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Al3+, Sm3+, Ag+, Na+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Zn2+ and Mn2+) in the form of nitrates (100 mM) was added to a solution of TC (2.0 mL, 0.1 mM) dissolved in H2O:CH3OH (80:20%, v/v). Although we have tested different combinations of solvent mixture for the recognition of metal ions by TC, only for this proportion of solvent mixture H2O:CH3OH (80:20%, v/v), a clear distinction of detection for Al3+ by TC was observed. The solution mixture was stirred before recording the fluorescence spectra in order to observe whether TC has the ability to change the spectral behavior. For the consistency of the results, all the experiments were performed in triplicate form at room temperature. In the next step, in the interference study (competitive binding analysis), during the detection of specific cation, the interference from other coexistent cation that could modify the intensity of fluorescence generated by the mixture [TC with Al3+] was studied, i.e., the sensitivity of receptor TC (0.1 mmol) for the recognition of Al3+ (0.1 mM) was analyzed in a competitive medium. Finally, in the titration study, typically, for each subsequent addition of aluminium nitrate (10 μL, 0.0–0.025 M) to TC (2.0 mL, 0.1 mmol), the spectral intensity was measured, and then plotted against the concentration of Al3+ ion. In the stoichiometry analysis, the binding of TC with Al3+ was also determined by Job’s method; a set of solutions containing a constant total concentration of receptor with guest ion were prepared in such way that the solutions have different proportions of TC and Al3+. In the plot, for TC, the maximum intensity that corresponds to the concentration of cation has been assigned to stoichiometric ratio for [TC:Al3+].

Milk samples (Alpura milk) were collected from milk industry, Mexico, and treated with HNO3 and H2SO4 to remove lipid moieties, then filtered off the precipitate and stored at −20 °C for the study. To the filtrate solution (25 mL supernatant), Al3+ (100 mmol) was added, followed by TC (100 mmol), so that the ratio of aluminium to TC were 1:1. Fluorescence was recorded for the resulting mixture solution. Similarly, for each addition of TC (10 μL) to the milk solution (2.0 mL), the intensity of fluorescence was measured until reaching the maximum intensity.

Yeast inocula in aqueous solutions and milk samples: Both yeast strains (Saccharomyces cerevisiae, or Debaryomyces spp.) were grown separately on Mueller-Hinton broth at 25 °C for 24 h. Each suspension was prepared by adding a loop full of cells to peptonised water (0.7% NaCl and 0.1% peptone in 1000 mL of water). Four solutions were prepared separately: (1) Blank: the cell suspension (1.0 mL) was poured into 1.0 mL of water; (2) TC (1000 μL, 100 μM) into 1.0 mL of cells; (3) solution of Al3+ (100 μL, 100 mM) mixed with 1.0 mL of cells; (4) Mixture solution of TC (1000 μL, 100 μM) and Al3+ (100 μL, 100 mM) with 1.0 mL of cells suspension. So, all the tubes contain cells suspensions of yeast (1000 μL). For each solution, the total volume was 2.0 mL.

In the same way as indicated (see Suppl Mat.), solutions were prepared but using treated milk, i.e. (1) Cells suspensions (1.0 mL) having milk (blank); (2) TC (1000 μL, 100 μM) together with cells suspensions and milk; (3) Treated milk having Al3+ (100 μL, 100 mM) and cells; (4) Treated milk having both TC (1000 μL, 100 μM) and Al3+ (100 μL, 100 mM) along with cells. So each solution (100 μL) mentioned above was taken for confocal microscopy studies, and all experiments were carried out in triplicate for consistency.

The recognition of Al3+ by using tetracycline was analyzed in Saccharomyces cerevisiae, or Debaryomyces spp.; for these studies, Muller Hinton agar as growth medium, and meat peptone and glucose as supplementary growth factors were used. Confocal fluorescence cell imaging was taken at different conditions by a laser scanning confocal microscope (Leica Microsystems, TCS SP5, Wetzlar, Germany) coupled with a blue diode laser (50 mW, excitation at 405 nm), and the laser scanning speed was about 400 Hz. The performance of biological tissue with the fluorescent responses was recorded between 450 and 550 nm.

The cell culture procedure is followed as reported elsewhere (Garcia-Gutierrez, Huerta-Aguilar, Thangarasu, & Vazquez-Ramos, 2017). First, the isolated cells (0.5 g) were poured into distilled water (5.0 mL) having sucrose [50 mg/mL] and it was allowed to grow for two hours before the dispersion of culture samples (250.0 µL) into Petri dishes in which the mixture of Muller Hinton agar, peptone and glucose (1.0% w/v each) was present. In the study, a solution of TC (5.0 mM) prepared in a mixture of solvents (H2O: MeOH 80:20) was added to the culture and then monitored the performance of the cells by fluorescence microscopy. To the petri dishes of having the growth medium, the antibiotic TC (250 μL) was added, and then homogenized for 10 min in order to diffuse completely into agar. Lastly, the yeast cells have been transferred from the test tubes to petri dishes for culture, and incubated for 24 h at 30° C. Similarly, Debaryomyces cells were also grown as mentioned above, typically, Debaryomyces spp. was maintained at 4 °C in Mueller-Hinton broth supplied with 0.3% sodium chloride and 0.3% dextrose.

Generally, in the recognition studies, the detection of metal ions has been first examined by using TC, for which the following cations were employed (Cd2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Al3+, Sm3+, Ag+, Na+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Zn2+ and Mn2+) (Fig. 1 a). Typically, each cationic solution in the form of nitrates (100 μL, 100 mM) was added to the solution of TC (2.0 mL, 100 μM) dissolved in H2O: CH3OH (80:20 %v/v), and then stirred to mix thoroughly before measuring the fluorescent emission (360–750 nm). After the excitation of the solution at 365 nm, the intensity of fluorescence emission was measured at 480 nm for each addition of metal ions, and the results show that only for the addition of Al3+, the characteristic of fluorescence was considerably changed with enhancing its intensity; this indicates that TC can perform as chemo-sensor for the detection of Al3+. This is consistent with the competitive analysis (Fig. 1b and c), where there is no interference from the coexistent cations, observing that the florescence intensity originated from the interaction of Al3+ ion with TC has not been altered. After analyzing the results, it was seen that the fluorescence intensity was not affected by adding other cations as no interference was observed from other cations except a small quenching caused by Cr3+. In the study, these cations (Cd2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Al3+, Sm3+, Ag+, Na+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Zn2+ and Mn2+) are being considered for the interference test, and thus each cation was added to a solution of TC (0.1 mM, 2.0 mL) having Al3+ (100.0 μL, 100.0 mM) in H2O/CH3OH (80:20, %v/v). The whole solution mixture was first stirred for 15 min. before reaching equilibrium (5.0 min.), and then the fluorescence emission was recorded at 480 nm after exciting at 365 nm. Although Fe3+ quenches the fluorescence significantly interfering in the detection of Al3+ because of its high affinity with polar solvents, it performs as reversible oxidizing agent towards the ligand. Moreover, Fe(III) can easily form Fe(OH)3 [18]. So, the quenching process is generally believed to be dynamic and common due to photoinduced electron transfer towards the excited state of the ligand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Titration of TC vs. Al3+ studies
ce:section.ce:para: The spectral titration of TC against the addition of Al3+ was carried out, and the behavior of fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 1). Briefly, for each subsequent addition of Al3+ ion (10 μL, 0.0–0.08 mM) to TC solution (2.0 mL, 0.1 mM) in H2O:MeOH (80:20%), the measured intensity (at 480 nm) was plotted against the concentration of Al3+, seeing that the intensity was steadily increased up to 0.02 mM, where it reached its maximum and then it was stabilized. It means, TC has an ability to detect Al3+ in any systems including environmental and biological samples because TC interacts specifically with that metal ion without interference from other metal ions. So the stoichiometry ratio for the interaction of TC with Al3+ determined by Job́s method (Fig. 1) was 1:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots derived from tetracycline as sensor for Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were synthesised from tetracycline (CDs) using the hydrothermal method at 120 °C as reported elsewhere (Sidhu, Mayank, Pandiyan, Kaur, & Singh, 2017). TC and ethylenediamine were dissolved in water (20 mL), and the mixture was stirred for 30 min, and subjected to hydrothermal process using an autoclave for 3 h. After thermal heating, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm to remove all sedimented particles. Ethylenediamine is attached on the CDsTC to improve the photo-luminescence properties by adding an –NH2 as a functional group on dots surface. (Fig. 2 ).

CDsTC were also synthesized using TC as carbon source and were characterized by different spectroscopic techniques (Fig. 2). Elemental analysis of CDs TC is as follows C 60.05%; H, 6.72%; N, 16.43%, and in the X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), the appearance of a broad diffraction peak is centered at 21.8° indicating the formation of carbon dots (CDs), and the peak at 42.8° (CDs TC) corresponds to the existence of small amount of graphite and it is consistent with the literature reports (Sidhu et al., 2017) (Fig. 2). Morphology of dots was analyzed by High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), showing that the particles are uniformly dispersed in solution, and the diameter was 10–15 nm (CDs). The stability of the CDs depends mainly on the nature of the environment, because the poor stability of CDs involves an aggregation, affecting the fluorescence properties. It was seen that in the absence of light no changes/shifts were observed in the fluorescence peak within 24 h; however, with the presence of light a decrease in fluorescence intensity was noticed.

Similarly, the metal recognition binding tests were performed for CDsTC using the following cations were employed (Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Al3+, Sm3+, Ag+, Na+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Zn2+ and Mn2+) (Fig. 2, iv a). The intensity of fluorescence emission was measured at 480 nm for each addition of metal ions, and the study reveals that the characteristic of fluorescence was significantly changed after the addition of Al3+; this indicates that CDsTC can also perform as chemo-sensor for the detection of Al3+, agreeing with the competitive analysis (Fig. 2, iv b), that no interference from the coexistent cations except Ag+ was observed. So the fluorescence spectral titration of CDsTC against the addition of Al3+ was studied, observing that the intensity was increased (Fig. 2, iv c). In the same way, OTC was used for the recognition of cations through (i) Metal ion binding tests, (ii) Analysis of interference from other cations in competitive medium (competitive binding test), and (iii) Guest–Host titration study OTC was employed to detect Al ion, showing that it detects Al3+ efficiently as TC (see Suppl. Mat) with a small interference from Cr3+ ion.

To the treated milk solution (25 mL, supernatant), Al3+ ion (100 mmol) was added followed by TC (100 mmol), so that the ratio of aluminium and TC was to be 1:1. For the resulting mixture, the fluorescence intensity was measured. Similarly, for each addition of TC (10 μL) to the milk solution (2.0 mL) having Al3+ (100 μL), the intensity of the signal was measured until reaching the maximum. The results show that only if milk has Al ion together with TC exhibit high fluorescence intensity. Therefore, this system can be applied to measure Al ion in milk by also adding TC; in the same way, but reversely, TC can be estimated by adding Al ion. This is consistent with the TC titration against Al ion in milk, where the peak intensity is increased for each addition of Al ion to milk (Fig. 3 ).

With using 3σ method, the limit of detection (LOD) of TC, CDTCs for the sensing of Al3+ was determined, and the data from three independent experiments were used in the study:
(1)
D e t e c t i o n
l i m i t
( D L )
=
3 σ
m
cc
σ
= standard deviation from triplicate experiments; mcc = slope of the calibration curve.

The LOD results are: 0.00056 mM for Al3+ by TC in H2O:MeOH (80:20%); 0.00022 mM for Al3+ by TC in in milk; 0.00050 mM for Al3+ by CDsTC in H2O:MeOH (80:20%); 0.00065 mM for Al3+ by OTC in H2O:MeOH (80:20%).

Confocal microscopy was used to analyze Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Debaryomyces spp. yeast cells after culture for 24 h, observing Saccharomyces cerevisiae presenting an oval shape, so the isolated cells were re-cultured to get uniform cells. Separately, the cells with TC as well as cells with Al3+ were cultured, and the performance of the treated cells was also analyzed through confocal microscopy. In the same way, the growth of cultured cells with the mixture of TC and Al3+ was observed (Suppl. Mat). The results show that the growth rate in cultures remained unchanged, implying that there was budding of cells at the right time since TC did not show toxicity towards Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Cells were grown, and then TC was added followed by Al3+ before observing the images by confocal microscopy; it was observed that the fluorophore (TC + Al3+) was present within the cells, as seen in the fluorescent images (Fig. 4 ). In those microorganisms that grew only with TC, without Al3+ or with Al3+ without TC, no fluorescent response was observed (Suppl. Mat). The fluorescence images were seen only for the cells having TC and Al3+, showing that the interaction of TC with Al3+ is essential to produce the fluorescence images.

The same experiments were carried out in milk, i.e. the interaction of Saccharomyces cerevisiae or Debaryomyces spp. with aluminum ion along with TC was studied in milk, showing that only in the cells grown with TC and aluminum (Suppl. Mat), a significant enhancement fluorescence image was seen because of TC interaction with a metal ion. Thus, TC can be applied as a potential chemo-sensor for the recognition of Al3+ in biological systems as well as milk samples. The work is compared with the published results, showing the better recovery and good detection limit (Table 1 ).

Full optimization of TC was first performed by DFT calculations using Gaussian-09 at B3LYP with the DGDZVP as basis set (Hay & Wadt, 1985) for [Al(TC)(OH)4]+. The data obtained for TC were used as input for the optimization of aluminium-TC complex. In the frontier molecular orbital (MOs) study, the energies of the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) were obtained for TC and also for its Al-complex. Since the MOs study would give a complete understanding of the bonding nature of aluminium complex with TC (M-L), it was used to analyze the behavior of fluorescence intensity for the complex. Natural Population Analysis (NPA) was used to analyze the charge distribution over donor atoms of TC (Sathya, Dhamodharan, & Dhandapani, 2017).

With using the condensed-to-atom Fukui indexes (CAFI) f(r) (Parr & Yang, 1984), the electronic density ρ(r) with the number of electrons N at a constant external potential v(r) was calculated to identify the active sites in the tetracycline as indicated below:
f
r
=
∂ ρ
r
∂ N

ρ

r

ρ
HOMO
r
;
f 0
r
≈
1 2
ρ
HOMO
r
+
ρ
LUMO
r

The electron density of the atoms in TC was determined with taking into account of the density of the first unoccupied molecular orbital ρ LUMO (r) as well as the density of the highest occupied molecular orbital ρ HOMO (r). The condensed Fukui indexes (f+k, f−k and f°k) are directly related to electrophilic or nucleophilic attacks as follows:
f
x
-
=
q x

r a d i c a l
a t t a c k

The results indicate that these reactive sites (C47, C28, C29) are vulnerable for electrophilic attack f − (r) in TC, and for nucleophilic attack f+(r), these are the active sites O21, N39, O44, O46. This is in agreement with the Mulliken analysis, where the charge density was significantly altered if TC is coordinated with Al ion, observing the excess electron density of the heteroatoms is transferred to the metal atom for the formation of a bond between TC and aluminium ion (see Suppl. Mat.), and it evidences the charge densities at O (−0.213) and O2 (−0.170) in the TC are being changed to −0.269 and −0.093 after the complex formation.

The optimized structure of [AlTC(OH)4)] (Fig. 5 ) was optimized by DFT at B3LYP/DGDZVP level of theory and the data (Suppl Mat) indicate that Al (III) sites in highly distorted octahedral geometry because the bond angles for the trans-oriented atoms around Al ion are greatly deviated from 180: 164.54° for O62-Al63-O46, 150.74° for O47-Al63-065, and 136.58° for O65-Al63-O48. Similarly, the bond angles for the cis-oriented atoms vary from 62.27 to 105.9°, deviating significantly from 90°. The bond distances are compared with several aluminium complexes of different ligands, showing that the Al-Nimine bond distance is shorter than those observed for other bonds as follows Al-O < Al-imine < Al-Br (see Suppl. Mat).

Density of States (DOS) for TC and [TC-Al3+] systems have been studied to show the existence of a direct impact with energy gap between single states (ΔE HOMO-LUMO). In the study, ΔE obtained for TC was 3.74 eV while for TC-Al, it was decreased to 3.49 eV, especially, the lowering of HOMO energy greatly discourages the transfer of electrons from HOMO (fluorophore) to HOMO of excited states, and consequently, the PET process inhibits electron transfer in order to increase the fluorescence intensity, allowing the electron transfer between the excited fluorophore and the receptor molecules is generally quenching the intensity.

The molecular orbitals (MOs) analysis confirms the bond formation between TC and the metal ion through overlapping of the orbitals from TC with those of the aluminium ion. For example, the HOMO orbitals are derived mainly from [Al(III)] with the π/p orbitals of TC, and obtained the following orbitals HOMO to HOMO-x; X = 0–17) and LUMO to LUMO + y (Y = 0–6) (Suppl. Mat.). Using molecular orbital data, the hardness (ƞ), related to the distribution of the electron density was also calculated for TC and its complexes as i.e., ƞ = (LUMO-HOMO)/2 for TC = −1.871 eV and Al complex = 1.749. For instance, the ΔE values are = −27.989 eV for [AlTC(OH)4)], showing that the charge transfer is energetically favorable if η ˃ 0 and ΔE < 0. Δ
E =
μ
2
2 η
(µ = chemical potential; η = chemical hardness of the ligand), and
μ = -
I + A
2
(I = ionization potential (IP); A = electron affinity (EA).

The antibiotic TC has been used as a chemo-sensor for the detection of Al3+ in milk as well as in the bio-samples Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Debaryomyces spp., producing cell images in confocal laser scanning microscopy. It has been found that there is no interference from coexisting cations (Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Sm3+, Ag+, Na+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Zn2+ and Mn2+) during the recognition of Al3+. TC performs competently as fluorescent probe for Al3+ ion in milk products as well as in living cells. Therefore, this technique can be applicable to determine aluminium or TC in milk products and also in bio-samples. In the molecular orbital study, it has been proved that the lowering of HOMO energy discourages the transfer of electrons from HOMO (fluorophore) to HOMO of excited states of Al-complex. Finally, tetracycline based carbon dots (CDs TC) is used efficiently to detect Al3+ ion in the presence of Ag2 +.
There are none.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Dirección General de Asuntos de Personal Académico (Project PAPIIT No IN209616) and CONACYT-DST (No. 266150) for the economic support; USAII, Faculty of Chemistry, UNAM for instrumental supports, and DGSCA, UNAM for computation facilities. Jesús Alfredo Ortega Granados thanks CONACYT for scholarship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2018.11.086.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
